ruleset.name: The Stewpot
ruleset.edition: The Stewpot (Tim Hutchings, slow-life community-building hack of Avery Alder's & Joe Mcdaldno-adjacent Stonetop / PbtA traditions). Inspired by Hutchings' published stewpot-style village games; this is a clean-room mechanic re-implementation, not the published rules.
ruleset.license: Original mechanics by Tim Hutchings & the broader Stonetop / PbtA tradition. This ruleset file is a clean-room re-implementation of a slow-life resolution mechanic adapted to the stance-modal-pool mode for the Marinara-RPG framework; no published Stewpot prose, NPC names, or village setting text is reproduced. Buy Hutchings' work to see the real thing.
ruleset.summary: Slow-life cozy village RPG. PCs are villagers who never leave on adventures — they stew. Each villager has one stat (Capability, 2-5) and chooses a stance per roll: ACTION (succeed OVER Capability — making something happen) or STILLNESS (succeed UNDER Capability — observing, recovering, holding space). Roll 2d6, +1d if invested in the activity. Exact-match faces are 'Perfect Days' — a beat of true grace the GM honors with a quiet good thing.

dice.notation: Pd6 stance=ACTION|STILLNESS vs Capability (2-3 dice typical)

stances.label: STILLNESS
stances.description: When the villager is observing, listening, recovering, holding space, letting a thing be. Each die UNDER Capability is a moment of grace counted toward the outcome. Use STILLNESS for: tending a garden patiently, sitting with a grieving neighbor, mending a torn cloak, watching the bread rise.

stances.label: ACTION
stances.description: When the villager is making a thing happen — pushing, persuading, doing. Each die OVER Capability is a forward step counted toward the outcome. Use ACTION for: persuading the council, hauling the harvest, raising a roof beam, putting out the fire before it spreads.

exactMatch.name: PERFECT DAY
exactMatch.effect: A die rolling EXACTLY Capability is a Perfect Day — a small grace. It counts as a success toward the outcome tier, AND the GM should narrate a quiet good thing: a kindness returned, an unexpected smile, a memory surfacing at the right moment. The Perfect Day reminds the table that the stewpot is the point, not the broth.

outcomeTiers.label: stalled
outcomeTiers.narration: Nothing moves. The thing the villager hoped for does not happen today; perhaps a worse thing does. The GM names what's lost — a chance, a feeling, a name — and the table sits with it for a moment before the next scene.

outcomeTiers.label: small-grace
outcomeTiers.narration: The villager gets a partial — the thing happens but smaller, slower, or at a quiet cost. A neighbor still grumbles, or the garden takes another day. The fiction moves, but barely.

outcomeTiers.label: good-day
outcomeTiers.narration: A good day. The thing happens cleanly. The villager goes home tired in the right way. The GM names one small joy that came with the success.

outcomeTiers.label: harvest
outcomeTiers.narration: A harvest — the rarest tier. The thing happens AND ripples outward. A neighbor benefits. A long-held tension softens. The stewpot is fuller tonight than it was this morning. GM names the second-order good.

attributes.name: Capability
attributes.description: Your one stat, 2-5. High Capability favors ACTION (over) — you are good at making things happen. Low Capability favors STILLNESS (under) — you are good at noticing, holding, receiving. The middle (3 or 4) is the table's most common villager.

skills.name: Garden
skills.description: Tending plants, reading weather, knowing what wants to grow here. Mark when invested in a gardening scene to add +1d to the pool.

skills.name: Cook
skills.description: Stewpot-keeping. Bread, broth, the small magic of feeding people. Mark when invested to add +1d.

skills.name: Mend
skills.description: Patching cloth, fixing fence, putting a thing back together that wanted to fall apart. Mark when invested to add +1d.

skills.name: Listen
skills.description: Hearing what neighbors aren't saying. Noticing the absent, the grieving, the new. Especially potent under STILLNESS. Mark when invested to add +1d.

skills.name: Persuade
skills.description: Asking neighbors for help, asking the council to vote your way, asking the storm to break later. Especially potent under ACTION. Mark when invested to add +1d.

derivedStats.name: Hearth
derivedStats.formula: narrative resource: how settled the villager is at home. Refill on Perfect Days; spend on rough scenes the GM calls for.

diceTagFormat.template: [mrrp-roll: ruleset=stewpot, stance={stance}, stat=Capability, statValue={statValue}, pool={pool}, dice=[{diceCsv}], successes={successes}, exactMatches={exactMatches}, tier={tier}{narrationHookPart}]
diceTagFormat.example: [mrrp-roll: ruleset=stewpot, stance=stillness, stat=Capability, statValue=3, pool=3, dice=[1,2,3], successes=3, exactMatches=1, tier=harvest, narrationHook=perfect_day]

lorebook.name: The Stewpot Rules Reference
lorebook.description: Keyword-triggered rules reference for the GM agent running a cozy village game in The Stewpot (Tim Hutchings tradition). Each entry surfaces only when its keys appear in recent narration or player input, so the GM gets the right rule at the right moment without inflating every prompt.

entries.name: Stance Toggle (Stillness vs Action)
entries.content: Every roll picks a stance the villager chooses BEFORE the dice land. STILLNESS counts dice UNDER Capability — for tending the garden patiently, sitting with a grieving neighbor, mending a torn cloak, watching bread rise. ACTION counts dice OVER Capability — for persuading the council, hauling the harvest, raising a beam, putting out the fire before it spreads. High Capability (4-5) favors ACTION; low Capability (2-3) favors STILLNESS. The same Capability is used in both stances — the stance picks the direction of grace, not the stat. Encourage players to consider which mode fits the fiction before reaching for dice; many scenes need stillness more than action.

entries.name: Perfect Days (Exact Match)
entries.content: A die rolling EXACTLY the villager's Capability is a PERFECT DAY. It counts as a success toward the outcome tier, AND the GM should narrate a quiet good thing: a kindness returned, an unexpected smile, a half-forgotten memory surfacing at the right moment, a song heard through an open window. Perfect Days are the heart of The Stewpot — they remind the table that the stewpot is the point, not the broth. Honor them every time. Don't undercut a Perfect Day with sarcasm or with a beat that immediately spoils it; let the grace land.

entries.name: Outcome Tiers (Stalled, Small Grace, Good Day, Harvest)
entries.content: Count successes (dice on the correct side of Capability for the chosen stance, plus any Perfect Day exact-matches). 0 successes = STALLED: nothing moves, perhaps a worse thing happens; the GM names what is lost — a chance, a feeling, a name — and the table sits with it. 1 success = SMALL GRACE: the thing happens partial / slow / at a quiet cost; a neighbor still grumbles, the garden takes another day, but the fiction moves. 2 successes = GOOD DAY: the thing happens cleanly; the villager goes home tired in the right way; the GM names one small joy that came along with it. 3+ successes = HARVEST: rarest tier; the thing happens AND ripples outward — a neighbor benefits, a long-held tension softens, the stewpot is fuller tonight; the GM names the second-order good.

entries.name: Helping Neighbors
entries.content: Helping is the only way to grow a villager's dice pool beyond the base 2 + invested bonus. A second villager (or a named NPC neighbor) describes how they assist in the fiction, and the player rolling adds +1d to their pool. There is no cap on helpers in principle, but in practice 2-3 is the maximum the table can credibly stage. Helping isn't free — the helper takes part in the consequences if the roll stalls. Use helping to make scenes about community, not solo achievement; The Stewpot is a slow-life game about the village, and helping is the central mechanism that makes that true.

entries.name: Seasonal Cycles and the Hearth
entries.content: Stewpot campaigns turn on seasons rather than missions. The GM names the season at the start of each session — spring planting, summer travel, autumn harvest, winter holding — and the season colors what scenes the table reaches for. The Hearth track (max 5, default 3) is each villager's quiet measure of how settled they are at home. Perfect Days refill the Hearth. Rough scenes spend Hearth — a death in the village, a long argument with a neighbor, a hard frost on tender plants. Empty Hearth means the villager needs a rest scene before more dice roll; this is a feature, not a bug. Festivals (the GM names one or two a year) refill all Hearths and reset season-long tensions.

entries.name: Invested Skills
entries.content: A villager is INVESTED in a skill they took at character creation (Garden, Cook, Mend, Listen, Persuade are the canonical five; campaigns add more). When a scene clearly leans on an invested skill, the player adds +1d to the pool. Invested skills are not all-purpose — a Cook helping the council is just persuading; a Persuader weeding the garden is just gardening. Invested skills also carry narrative weight: a Listener notices what others miss; a Mender's mended things hold longer; a Cook's stewpot tonight is a little better. Encourage the table to name an invested skill before rolling so the +1d is part of the fiction, not bookkeeping.

entries.name: What a Stewpot Game Is Not
entries.content: The Stewpot is NOT an adventure game. Villagers do not leave the village on quests. There are no monsters, no combats, no XP for kills, no treasure pulls. The drama is at-home and inward: who is grieving, who is harvesting, who is leaving the village (rarely, sadly, finally), who is joining (rarely, joyfully, awkwardly), what the council will decide, what is being mended. When players reach for adventure-game beats, the GM gently redirects to the slow-life frame — 'tell me about the morning before the council vote', 'what does your villager carry to the funeral', 'what does the stew need tonight'. The genre is the rule.

entries.name: Attribute: Capability (CAP)
entries.content: The Stewpot attribute. Capability (CAP) — Your one stat, 2-5. High Capability favors ACTION (over) — you are good at making things happen. Low Capability favors STILLNESS (under) — you are good at noticing, holding, receiving. The middle (3 or 4) is the table's most common villager.

entries.name: Skill: Garden
entries.content: The Stewpot skill. Garden. Tending plants, reading weather, knowing what wants to grow here. Mark when invested in a gardening scene to add +1d to the pool.

entries.name: Skill: Cook
entries.content: The Stewpot skill. Cook. Stewpot-keeping. Bread, broth, the small magic of feeding people. Mark when invested to add +1d.

entries.name: Skill: Mend
entries.content: The Stewpot skill. Mend. Patching cloth, fixing fence, putting a thing back together that wanted to fall apart. Mark when invested to add +1d.

entries.name: Skill: Listen
entries.content: The Stewpot skill. Listen. Hearing what neighbors aren't saying. Noticing the absent, the grieving, the new. Especially potent under STILLNESS. Mark when invested to add +1d.

entries.name: Skill: Persuade
entries.content: The Stewpot skill. Persuade. Asking neighbors for help, asking the council to vote your way, asking the storm to break later. Especially potent under ACTION. Mark when invested to add +1d.

entries.name: Derived stat: Hearth
entries.content: The Stewpot derived stat. Hearth — formula: narrative resource: how settled the villager is at home. Refill on Perfect Days; spend on rough scenes the GM calls for.

customTools.name: stewpot_reference
customTools.description: Returns canonical The Stewpot reference: dice, resolution, difficulty ladder, attributes, skills. Call when the GM needs authoritative ruleset detail mid-narration.
customTools.staticResult:
# The Stewpot — canonical reference

Slow-life cozy village RPG. PCs are villagers who never leave on adventures — they stew. Each villager has one stat (Capability, 2-5) and chooses a stance per roll: ACTION (succeed OVER Capability — making something happen) or STILLNESS (succeed UNDER Capability — observing, recovering, holding space). Roll 2d6, +1d if invested in the activity. Exact-match faces are 'Perfect Days' — a beat of true grace the GM honors with a quiet good thing.

## Dice
- Type: `d6`
- Notation: `Pd6 stance=ACTION|STILLNESS vs Capability (2-3 dice typical)`

## Resolution
- Mode: `stance-modal-pool`
- Pool formula: 2 + (invested ? 1 : 0) + (helping_neighbors)

## Attributes
- **Capability** (CAP) — Your one stat, 2-5. High Capability favors ACTION (over) — you are good at making things happen. Low Capability favors STILLNESS (under) — you are good at noticing, holding, receiving. The middle (3 or 4) is the table's most common villager.

## Skills
- **Garden** — Tending plants, reading weather, knowing what wants to grow here. Mark when invested in a gardening scene to add +1d to the pool.
- **Cook** — Stewpot-keeping. Bread, broth, the small magic of feeding people. Mark when invested to add +1d.
- **Mend** — Patching cloth, fixing fence, putting a thing back together that wanted to fall apart. Mark when invested to add +1d.
- **Listen** — Hearing what neighbors aren't saying. Noticing the absent, the grieving, the new. Especially potent under STILLNESS. Mark when invested to add +1d.
- **Persuade** — Asking neighbors for help, asking the council to vote your way, asking the storm to break later. Especially potent under ACTION. Mark when invested to add +1d.
